bio.title: Judge
bio.photo_notes: Portrait of James Hooker (1792-1858), Surrogate of Dutchess County NY.

institutions.institution_blurb: Yale College Class of 1810. One of many Hooker-line Yale graduates spanning generations.
Judge — already wired in earlier stage.

education.notes: Admitted to bar in Poughkeepsie 1813 after Litchfield.

career.notes: Admitted to the bar in Poughkeepsie 1813.

career.organization: New York State Erie Canal Commission
career.notes: Appointed Canal Commissioner of New York.

narrative_blocks.header: His daughter's line became Gilded Age Manhattan royalty
narrative_blocks.body: James Hooker's daughter Catherine Livingston Hooker married Colonel John William Hamersley in 1840 — a Manhattan lawyer, traveler, and social figure who had met Muhammad Ali Pasha in Egypt and helped save the Mexican Republic from Napoleon — and their line grew into one of Gilded Age New York's most prominent families: the Colonel's son J. Hooker Hamersley published poetry, once courted Edith Roosevelt before she chose Theodore, and whose son Louis Gordon Hamersley was born to international headlines as the 'seven million dollar baby' in 1892. The Hooker who had been a Windsor merchant's son and Poughkeepsie probate lawyer thus became, within three generations, the progenitor of a Fifth Avenue dynasty that hosted a President's Cup speedboat ceremony and twelve hundred crippled veterans on a Hudson River estate.

narrative_blocks.header: Twelve years as Surrogate of Dutchess County
narrative_blocks.body: James Hooker served as Surrogate of Dutchess County for twelve years from 1828 to 1840 — the probate judge responsible for wills, estates, and guardianships across one of New York's most prosperous Hudson Valley counties — and was selected as Presidential Elector of New York in 1836, before being appointed Canal Commissioner of New York in 1842, overseeing operations of the Erie Canal that had transformed the American interior. He had come to Poughkeepsie in 1813 after graduating Yale (1810) and studying at Litchfield Law School, the first law school in the United States, and spent his entire career in Dutchess County, dying there in September 1858.

narrative_blocks.header: Vestryman of Christ Church for forty-one years
narrative_blocks.body: James Hooker served as Vestryman of Christ Church in Poughkeepsie from 1817 — the year after he married Helen Sarah Reade and settled in Dutchess County — until his death in 1858, forty-one consecutive years in the same Episcopal congregation through the entire arc of his career as Surrogate, Canal Commissioner, and retired counsellor. This was a quiet denominational shift: the Hookers were descendants of the Puritan Rev. Thomas Hooker, a Congregationalist founder of Hartford, yet James spent his adult life in the Episcopal church — a shift that would be echoed when his grandson J. Hooker Hamersley was eulogized at Grace Church on Fifth Avenue, the grandest Episcopal church in Manhattan.

narrative_blocks.header: He married into the Reade Street Livingstons
narrative_blocks.body: On January 24, 1816, James Hooker married Helen Sarah Reade in Poughkeepsie — the granddaughter of John Reade (1745-1808) and Catherine Livingston Reade, connecting the Windsor Hooker family to both the Livingston Manor dynasty and to the colonial New York merchant family whose ancestor Joseph Reade had served as warden of Trinity Church for fifty years, sat on the Governor's Provincial Council, and given Reade Street in Lower Manhattan its name. The marriage brought into the Hooker line a thread of colonial New York aristocracy that would eventually surface in his grandson J. Hooker Hamersley — a man who traced his ancestry to both Robert Livingston the Elder (grantee of Livingston Manor by royal charter) and the Reade family whose name still runs through Lower Manhattan.

The Hooker who had been a Windsor merchant's son and Poughkeepsie probate lawyer thus became, within three generations, the progenitor of a Fifth Avenue dynasty that hosted a President's Cup speedboat ceremony and twelve hundred crippled veterans on a Hudson River estate.

narrative_blocks.body: James Hooker served as Vestryman of Christ Church in Poughkeepsie from 1817 — the year after he married Helen Sarah Reade and settled in Dutchess County — until his death in 1858, forty-one consecutive years in the same Episcopal congregation through the entire arc of his career as Surrogate, Canal Commissioner, and retired counsellor. This was a quiet denominational shift: the Hookers were descendants of the Puritan Rev. Thomas Hooker, a Congregationalist founder of Hartford, yet James spent his adult life in the Episcopal church — a shift that would be echoed when his grandson J. Hooker Hamersley was eulogized at Grace Church on Fifth Avenue, the grandest Episcopal church in Manhattan.

narrative_blocks.header: He married into the Reade Street Livingstons
narrative_blocks.body: On January 24, 1816, James Hooker married Helen Sarah Reade in Poughkeepsie — the granddaughter of John Reade (1745-1808) and Catherine Livingston Reade, connecting the Windsor Hooker family to both the Livingston Manor dynasty and to the colonial New York merchant family whose ancestor Joseph Reade had served as warden of Trinity Church for fifty years, sat on the Governor's Provincial Council, and given Reade Street in Lower Manhattan its name. The marriage brought into the Hooker line a thread of colonial New York aristocracy that would eventually surface in his grandson J. Hooker Hamersley — a man who traced his ancestry to both Robert Livingston the Elder (grantee of Livingston Manor by royal charter) and the Reade family whose name still runs through Lower Manhattan.

bio.photo_notes: Sarah Helen Reade Hooker (1790-1879); wife of Judge James Hooker

narrative_blocks.header: Her family's name appears on a Manhattan street
narrative_blocks.body: Sarah Helen Reade was the granddaughter of John Reade (1745-1808) and Catherine Livingston Reade — a member of the great Livingston family of the Hudson Valley — and her great-grandfather was Joseph Reade (1694-1771), the New York merchant and provincial councillor whose family gave Reade Street in Lower Manhattan its name, serving as warden of Trinity Church for over fifty years and counting among his in-laws the Mayor of New York City (1702-1703) and Frederick Philipse, one of the wealthiest Dutch merchants in colonial New York. She was also, through her Livingston grandmother, a fifth-generation descendant of Robert Livingston the Elder, the Scottish immigrant granted Livingston Manor by royal charter — a lineage her grandson James Hooker Hamersley would proudly enumerate in his biography.

narrative_blocks.header: Her great-grandfather advised delaying the Stamp Act
narrative_blocks.body: Joseph Reade, Sarah's great-grandfather, sat on the New York Governor's Provincial Council from 1761 until his death in 1771, and — while serving under Governor Sir Henry Moore — advised the administration to delay the issuance of stamped paper required by the Stamp Act, positioning himself among the colonial establishment figures who helped defuse that crisis before revolution became inevitable. He also sat on the grand jury of the New York Slave Conspiracy trials of 1741, which based on questionable testimony resulted in death sentences for thirty-four defendants and the deportation of ninety-one others — a reminder that his distinguished family history also carried moral complexity at its center.

sources.label: Americana Aristocracy — Helen Sarah Reade Hooker

naming_inspiration.description: Middle name 'Livingston' honors her maternal grandmother Catherine Livingston Reade and the Livingston Manor family.

narrative_blocks.header: Daughter of a judge
narrative_blocks.body: Catherine Livingston Hooker was born in Poughkeepsie in 1817, the daughter of Judge James Hooker (Yale 1810, Erie Canal Commissioner) and Sarah Helen Reade, whose family gave Reade Street its name and descended from Robert Livingston the Elder — and she married Colonel John William Hamersley, a New York City lawyer and traveler who had met Muhammad Ali Pasha in Egypt, helped save the Mexican Republic from Napoleon, and whose Friday receptions at 255 Fifth Avenue drew the eminent writers and artists of the city. She was described by his obituary as 'a lady of rare abilities and sterling worth' — a Poughkeepsie judge's daughter who became a Manhattan society figure — and died in February 1867 at age forty-nine, leaving four children who would carry both the Hooker name and the Reade-Livingston ancestry into the Gilded Age.

sources.label: Edward Hooker, Descendants of Rev. Thomas Hooker (1909)

bio.title: Colonel
bio.photo_notes: Colonel John William Hamersley (1808-1889), Manhattan lawyer, traveler, and social figure

burial.plot_notes: Trinity Churchyard Financial District — Hamersley family vault per WikiTree

notable.notable_blurb: First American to meet Muhammad Ali Pasha

career.notes: Per EH: "He was a lawyer and an author."

narrative_blocks.header: Met the founder of modern Egypt in 1834
narrative_blocks.body: While traveling through the Middle East in 1834 — at a time when few Americans had crossed the Atlantic — Colonel John William Hamersley became one of the first Americans to meet Muhammad Ali Pasha, the Albanian-born ruler who built modern Egypt, beginning a career of extraordinary travel through Europe and Asia that included a presentation at the Court of Saint James. His travels inspired his granddaughter Catherine Livingston Hamersley to become the first American woman to visit Riyadh in Saudi Arabia and to be elected a Fellow of the Royal Geographical Society — the travel impulse proving genetic across generations.

narrative_blocks.header: He and Beekman helped save Mexico from Napoleon
narrative_blocks.body: Colonel Hamersley and James William Beekman organized a famous banquet in New York at which prominent Americans delivered speeches supporting the Mexican Republic against France's imposition of Emperor Maximilian — the ensuing expression of sympathy, Hamersley believed, influenced Congress to recognize the Republic and persuaded European nations to follow, forcing Napoleon's withdrawal and Maximilian's fall. He had obtained a promise from the Mexican minister that Maximilian's life would be spared, but popular Mexican clamor made it impossible; the effort nonetheless placed Hamersley at the center of one of the major diplomatic episodes of American Reconstruction-era foreign policy.

narrative_blocks.header: His Friday receptions at 255 Fifth became famous
narrative_blocks.body: Colonel Hamersley's Friday night receptions at 255 Fifth Avenue became celebrated in New York's literary and artistic world — he had helped found the Union Club, belonged to the Century and St. Nicholas clubs, collected a rare library rich in books from across Europe, translated Jacques Abbadie's 'A Chemical Change in the Eucharist' from French, and written several works of his own. His British friend Captain Mayne Reid, the adventure novelist, made him the hero of 'The Lone Ranche,' and his obituary described him as 'a man of noble presence and fine character, rugged in physique, capable of great labor, and noted for his religious conviction.'

sources.label: Edward Hooker, Descendants of Rev. Thomas Hooker (1909)

bio.photo_notes: J. Hooker Hamersley (1844-1901), Gilded Age lawyer, poet, and heir

burial.plot_notes: Trinity Church Cemetery and Mausoleum Hamilton Heights per FA; funeral at Grace Church

notable.notable_blurb: Gilded Age heir, lawyer, and poet

education.notes: Also BA Columbia College 1865 with high honors.
Graduated with high honors; oration at commencement exercises at the Academy of Music.

career.notes: Per EH: "He was a lawyer and poet as well as writer of various prose articles of historical interest."

institutions.institution_blurb: Columbia Law School 1867; practiced at the office of James W. Gerard, then leader of the New York bar; prosecuted precedent cases in Church Street opening litigation, winning in Court of Appeals.

landmarks.notes: 1030 Fifth Avenue mansion — site where his wife Margaret Willing Chisolm Hamersley died January 5, 1904

naming_inspiration.description: Middle name 'Hooker' honors his maternal grandfather James Hooker of Poughkeepsie and the Hooker lineage back to Rev. Thomas Hooker.

cross_connections.display_label: Cousins: Gilded Age poet and Army intelligence chief
cross_connections.notes: H03408 J. Hooker Hamersley (gen 7, great-grandchild of H00348 through H00797 Judge James Hooker) and HD4700 GV Strong (gen 8, great-great-grandchild of H00348 through H00795 Dolly Hooker Strong) are second cousins once removed. Both achieved national prominence in different eras.

cross_connections.display_label: Cousins: Gilded Age poet and Eisenhower's Treasury Secretary
cross_connections.notes: H03408 J. Hooker Hamersley (gen 7 through H00797) and HD4679 George Magoffin Humphrey (gen 8 through H00799 Mary Chaffee Hooker) are second cousins once removed — both great-grandchildren (at different removes) of H00348 Captain James Hooker.

narrative_blocks.header: Courted Edith Roosevelt before she chose Theodore
narrative_blocks.body: J. Hooker Hamersley was one of Edith Kermit Carow's suitors in the early 1880s, before she married Theodore Roosevelt in December 1886 — placing him in the drawing rooms of the same upper-class New York world where the future president was being received, a biographical footnote that hints at the social stratum he inhabited. He married Margaret Willing Chisolm in 1888 at the Chisolm mansion in Brooklyn, with Mary Schieffelin (granddaughter of William H.

narrative_blocks.header: His son's birth in 1892 made international headlines
narrative_blocks.body: When Louis Gordon Hamersley was born on July 21, 1892, the Hamersley coachman reportedly drove through Newport 'waving his hat frantically and shouting: Hip, hip, hurrah! We've got a seven million dollar baby!' — because Louis stood to inherit the $7 million fortune of his father's cousin Louis Carré Hamersley, held by the former Duchess of Marlborough and contingent on J. Hooker Hamersley producing a male heir.

narrative_blocks.header: Stepped aside for Astor; published The Seven Voices
narrative_blocks.body: Nominated for the New York State Assembly in 1877 by the Independent Republicans, J. Hooker Hamersley withdrew from the race in favor of his friend William Waldorf Astor — whom he then actively campaigned to elect — and turned his energy to law, real estate, and a writing career that produced 'The Seven Voices' (1898), a poetry collection whose verses included 'The Countersign,' 'Yellow Roses,' 'The Midnight Sun,' and 'Voice of the Breakers.' He died at his country estate 'Brookhurst' at Garrison-on-Hudson on September 15, 1901 at age fifty-seven; his wife died at 1030 Fifth Avenue, the Hamersley mansion, on January 5, 1904.

narrative_blocks.header: He donated the Children's Aid Society's main building
narrative_blocks.body: J. Hooker Hamersley was a long-time financial supporter of the Newsboys' Lodging House and the Children's Aid Society — so committed that when the Society opened its new Summer Home in Bath Beach, Brooklyn on June 18, 1895, the main building was his personal gift, while the dormitory cottages were donated by John Jacob Astor, Mrs. William Astor, and Mrs. William Waldorf Astor. The New York Times called it 'a picturesque little village, this vacation home for New York's little waifs, with beautiful views on sea and land in all directions,' and Hamersley's building was at its center; after his death in 1901, his children Louis and Catherine continued the family's support of the Newsboys' Lodging House and financed its Christmas celebrations every year.

sources.label: Edward Hooker, Descendants of Rev. Thomas Hooker (1909)

narrative_blocks.header: Married into the de Peyster-Field family of New
narrative_blocks.body: Virginia Hamersley married Cortlandt de Peyster Field, the son of Benjamin Hazard Field, connecting the Hooker-Hamersley line to both the de Peyster family (descended from Abraham de Peyster, New York City mayor and treasurer in the colonial era) and to the Field commercial dynasty of New York. She was named as a beneficiary in her father Colonel John William Hamersley's complex will, which became the subject of a landmark trust accounting case — Bank of New York and Trust Company v. Louis Gordon Hamersley — that reached the New York Appellate Division, testing the interpretation of a bequest clause through a generation of Hamersley litigation.

sources.label: Edward Hooker, Descendants of Rev. Thomas Hooker (1909)

naming_inspiration.description: Middle name 'Reade' honors the Reade family of New York City, whose name is on Reade Street in lower Manhattan.

narrative_blocks.header: Named Reade for her grandmother's Reade Street family
narrative_blocks.body: Helen Reade Hamersley was named for her maternal grandmother Sarah Helen Reade Hooker, whose family gave Reade Street in Lower Manhattan its name through their ancestor Joseph Reade (1694-1771), the provincial councillor and Trinity Church warden — making the name 'Reade' in Helen Reade Hamersley a deliberate act of genealogical memory stretching back to colonial New York. She married Charles D. Stickney Jr. and died in 1911 without issue, her share of her father's trust passing to her surviving siblings under the will's provisions.

sources.label: Edward Hooker, Descendants of Rev. Thomas Hooker (1909)

death.cause: postpartum complications — died nine days after giving birth to daughter Katherine

naming_inspiration.description: Named 'Livingston' to honor her grandmother Catherine Livingston Reade, descended from the first Lord of Livingston Manor.

narrative_blocks.header: Married a Livingston; died days after childbirth
narrative_blocks.body: Catherine Livingston Hamersley married John Henry Livingston of Clermont on November 2, 1871 — uniting the Hooker line with the Livingston family of Clermont, one of the great colonial New York dynasties descended from Robert Livingston, founder of Livingston Manor in the 17th century. She died on April 19, 1873, just nine days after giving birth to her daughter Katherine, who would carry on both the Hooker and Livingston lines.

sources.label: Edward Hooker, Descendants of Rev. Thomas Hooker (1909)

narrative_blocks.header: Died at eighteen
narrative_blocks.body: Helen Mary Hooker was born in Poughkeepsie on June 12, 1819 and died on January 24, 1838 at age eighteen — before her father's career as Canal Commissioner, before her older sister Catherine married Colonel Hamersley, and before the entire arc of the Gilded Age that her sister's family would inhabit. She left no descendants, while her sister Catherine's line extended through the J. Hooker Hamersley family into the 1970s, the Hooker name surviving through the Hamersley branch's middle names long after Helen Mary's early death in Poughkeepsie.

sources.label: Edward Hooker, Descendants of Rev. Thomas Hooker (1909)

cross_connections.display_label: His mother — daughter of Governor Joseph Talcott.
His maternal grandfather, Governor of Connecticut 1724-1741.
First cousin once removed via the Talcott family.
Wife of his cousin Wadsworth — also a Hooker descendant.
Father-in-law and partner in the Hooker and Chaffee firm.
his partner in the firm Hooker & Chaffee
brother of his wife Mary Chaffee

narrative_blocks.header: Hooker and Chaffee supplied the Continental Army
narrative_blocks.body: Hooker and Chaffee was one of New England's largest mercantile houses before the Revolution, transacting much business for the Colonial Government. The partners gave freely to the patriot cause and were seriously embarrassed at war's end.

narrative_blocks.header: His firm provisioned alongside Wadsworth's commissary
narrative_blocks.body: The Hooker and Chaffee firm operated alongside Jeremiah Wadsworth's larger commissary operation — both Hooker-Talcott descendants provisioning the same Continental Army. The Connecticut River Valley supply network was effectively a Hooker-tree internal collaboration.

narrative_blocks.header: His Windsor house is on the National Register
narrative_blocks.body: He built the James Hooker House ca. 1772 at 118 Palisado Avenue, Windsor — now part of the Palisado Avenue Historic District on the National Register of Historic Places. The interior was later gutted for Chaffee School classrooms but the Federal-period exterior survives under Greek Revival additions.

institutions.institution_blurb: Silent partner; one of New England's great Revolutionary-era trading houses.

landmarks.landmark_blurb: He built this house c.1772; it survives on Palisado Avenue's National Register district.

bio.title: Captain
bio.photo_notes: Portrait of Captain James Hooker (1742-1805), Windsor CT merchant.

career.notes: Principal of INST070 Hooker and Chaffee. Succeeded to father's Hartford business with brothers; established Windsor branch. See INST070 for full firm history.

parents.mother_research_notes: Lydia (Griswold) Chaffee, b.Dec 17, 1722 Simsbury CT; d.Oct 1, 1801 Windsor CT. Daughter of the Griswold family of Connecticut.

narrative_blocks.header: Brother John was partner in Hooker & Chaffee
narrative_blocks.body: Her older brother John Chaffee was a partner in the firm Hooker & Chaffee — the business linking her birth family directly to her husband's. The 1784 marriage to Captain James Hooker thus joined both personal and commercial lines of the Windsor Connecticut Chaffee household.

narrative_blocks.header: Lived 41 years past husband's 1805 death
narrative_blocks.body: She survived her husband Captain James Hooker by forty-one years — he died December 1805, she lived until October 1846 at eighty-six. After James's death she returned from Rochester New York to Windsor Connecticut and lived with her unmarried sister Hepsibah Chaffee until Hepsibah's 1834 death.

cross_connections.display_label: Both families joined through Talcott siblings.
His wife's brother Matthew also married into the Hooker family line.
Both tied to separate Talcott branches in the same family generation.
Both families joined through Talcott siblings a generation later.

narrative_blocks.header: Married Governor Talcott's daughter Eunice
narrative_blocks.body: Eunice Talcott was the second daughter of Joseph Talcott, Governor of Connecticut from 1725 to 1741. Their marriage directly joined the two founding Hartford families — reinforced when Eunice's brother Matthew simultaneously married Mary Russell, herself a Hooker descendant.

narrative_blocks.header: Named co-heir in the Governor's estate
narrative_blocks.body: On December 25, 1742, Nathaniel and Eunice appeared before the Court of Probate in Hartford to sign Governor Joseph Talcott's estate distribution as co-heirs. It was one of the final official acts binding the Hooker family to the colonial governor's wealth.

narrative_blocks.header: Yale man, militia captain, Assembly representative
narrative_blocks.body: He graduated Yale College in 1729 and held the rank of Captain in the Connecticut Militia as early as 1734. He served as Hartford's representative in the General Assembly across three sessions, while running a substantial Hartford mercantile estate.

narrative_blocks.header: Son James partnered in Hooker & Chaffee
narrative_blocks.body: His son James Hooker was a partner in the mercantile firm Hooker & Chaffee, which grew very successful during the Revolutionary War era. The firm continued the family's Hartford commercial standing into the next generation.

cross_connections.display_label: Sister-in-law — Mary Russell married her brother Matthew Talcott.

narrative_blocks.header: She and brother Matthew both married Hooker descendants
narrative_blocks.body: She married Captain Nathaniel Hooker — a direct Hooker-surname descendant. Her brother Matthew Talcott simultaneously married Mary Russell, herself a Hooker descendant through the Pierpont line. Two of Governor Joseph Talcott's children marrying into the Hooker family in the same generation is why "Talcott" became a permanent name in the Hooker tree.

cross_connections.display_label: Business partner in the Hooker and Chaffee mercantile firm.

narrative_blocks.header: Documented his enslaved Betty Stevenson by name
narrative_blocks.body: His 1791 statement to the Hartford town clerk recorded his ownership of 'a Negro Woman' and her newborn daughter 'called by the Name Betty Stevenson.' He registered the birth as legally required.

narrative_blocks.header: Held a high rank in Connecticut medicine
narrative_blocks.body: He came to Hartford to start a drug store and settled in Windsor in 1755 on the advice of Dr. Isaac Bull. He practiced there for sixty years until his death.

narrative_blocks.header: Born seven weeks after his father's death
narrative_blocks.body: His father Hezekiah Chaffee Sr. died in October 1731 and Hezekiah was born December 6 of the same year in Rehoboth, Massachusetts. He was the youngest of nine children.

INST022.primary_name: American Board of Commissioners for Foreign Missions
INST022.name: American Board of Commissioners for Foreign Missions
INST022.notes: First American foreign-mission organization. Tree connections: Rev. Elias Cornelius (agent 1816, secretary 1831-32); Ainsworth and Harriet Ellsworth Blunt, missionaries to the Cherokee at Brainerd, Tennessee; Eliza Talcott, missionary to Japan; Rev. William Banfield Capron, Madura mission; Judge John Hooker, member.

INST024.name: Litchfield Law School
INST024.notes:
Founded 1784 by Judge Tapping Reeve (X00614) — the first law school in the United States. Tapping Reeve married Sarah "Sally" Burr Reeve (H00912), 5th-generation Hooker descendant, daughter of Esther Edwards Burr (H00378). Aaron Burr Jr. (H00913) — 5th-generation Hooker descendant — studied law here under his brother-in-law Tapping Reeve before enlisting in 1775. Among Litchfield's other notable students: John C. Calhoun (also became VP — two Vice Presidents produced by one law school). The school ran until 1833; at its peak enrolled students from across the country. Tapping Reeve House and Law School designated National Historic Landmark, 1965. In 1781, Reeve (with Theodore Sedgwick) represented Elizabeth Freeman ("Bett") in Brom & Bett v. Ashley — case that led to abolition of slavery in Massachusetts. Reeve also brought Rev. Lyman Beecher (father of Harriet Beecher Stowe) to Litchfield. The Litchfield Law School is among the most consequential institutions connected to the Hooker tree.
[hooker_connection] founded by in-law of Hooker descendant; Hooker descendant studied here
